tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.title: Complex instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 7 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 72 FP arithmetical operations:
  • 48: addition or subtraction (16 inside FMA instructions)
  • 24: multiply (16 inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 408 bytes (51 double precision FP elements). The binary loop is storing 64 bytes (8 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.15 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Proportion of vectorizable instructions that are actually vectorized. Higher is better.
expert.title: Vectorization ratios

expert.help: Effective/average relative length/width used in vector/SIMD registers. Higher is better.
expert.title: Vector efficiency ratios

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 6.33 cycles. At this rate:
  • 67% of peak load performance is reached (64.42 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 15% of peak store performance is reached (10.11 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

header: 47% of peak computational performance is used (11.37 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.details: All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt: Your loop is fully vectorized, using full register length.

gain.workaround:
• Read less array elements
  • Write less array elements
  • Provide more information to your compiler:
    • hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by:
  • reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck)
  • writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck)
By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 6.33 to 5.00 cycles (1.27x speedup).

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 16 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
